title: Lecture 7 Greeks updated
body:
Derivatives The Greek letters  Topics

• Hedging against factors affecting options prices • Greek letters: delta, gamma, vega, theta, and rho • Riskiness of options and option moneyness • Using Greeks for managing portfolio risks • Greeks for European vs. American options

Remember this table from previous lectures? Factors affecting the value of the option (premium) Option premium Factors European European American American call, c put, p call, C put, P Current stock price, S0 + ‒ + ‒ Strike price, K ‒ + ‒ + Time to maturity, T ? ? + + Volatility of stock price, σ + + + + Risk-free rate for maturity T, r + ‒ + ‒ Dividends, D ‒ + ‒ +

Factors affecting the value of an option

• We now want to discuss these effects more carefully by considering the partial derivatives: 𝜕𝑓 , 𝜕𝑓 , 𝜕𝑓 , and 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑟 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝜎 • We’ll also look at the second derivative, 𝜕 2𝑓 • Here, f is the value of the option; but these derivatives could be applied to the value of any derivative security • We saw some of these derivatives when we 2 looked at the Black-Scholes partial 1 2 𝑑 𝑓 = 𝑟𝑓 differential equation: 𝑑𝑓 + 𝑟𝑆 𝑑𝑓 + 𝜎 2 𝑆 2 𝑑 𝑑𝑆 2 𝑑𝑆 • We want to look at Taylor’s Theorem, which says that: 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2 𝑓 2 ∆𝑓 = ∆𝑡 + ∆𝑆 + ∆𝜎 + ∆𝑟 + 2 (∆𝑆) + ⋯ 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝜎 𝜕𝑟 2 𝜕𝑆

The Greek letters

• We want to look at Taylor’s Theorem, which says that: 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2 𝑓 2+ ⋯ ∆𝑓 = ∆𝑡 + ∆𝑆 + ∆𝜎 + ∆𝑟 + (∆𝑆) 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝜎 𝜕𝑟 2 𝜕𝑆 2 • We name these derivatives as follows ∆𝑓 = 𝑇ℎ𝑒𝑡𝑎 × ∆𝑡 + 𝐷𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 × ∆𝑆 + 𝑉𝑒𝑔𝑎 × ∆𝜎 + 𝑅ℎ𝑜 × ∆𝑟 + 𝐺𝑎𝑚𝑚𝑎 × (∆𝑆)2 + ⋯ • We can use this relationship to hedge an option or a portfolio value against fluctuations in these factors • Why do we want to do that? Because these factors affect the value of the option we hold (and the whole portfolio)

Who uses Greek letters for hedging?

• Anyone who has a significant position in options may consider calculating all these Greek letters to hedge against fluctuating factors • Who will hold significant positions in options? • Option traders, but more importantly, option market makers • Option market makers • Often small specialized firms with a seat at the exchange • Their key business is to make profits from bid-ask spreads, which are wide in options • They hold inventory of many options, i.e., they bear significant inventory risks (overnight) • They incur significant costs for hedging their positions

Option markets

• Most option markets (e.g., CBOE) have designated market makers (assigned by an exchange) who are required to constantly quote bid and ask prices in certain options • For doing so, option market makers are getting reduced commissions from exchanges, additional rebates, and other benefits • Let’s look at what ASX requires from market makers in Australia (link) • Whenever you trade in options, most likely you trade with a market maker • Market makers constantly monitor the markets and update their option quotes: • Based on market and asset-specific information they get • Based on changes in their inventory (how many long and short positions do they hold?) • Based on existing buying and selling demands • If they don’t do that, they are at risk of trading with an informed trader (making losses)

The role of market makers

• Market makers facilitate trading between counterparties • Without market makers, option markets could turn illiquid and there would be not much trades • They make option prices informationally efficient by updating their quotes • They also stabilize prices, since they have requirements to post quotes (even if markets are bad for that) • Without market makers prices could vary widely over short intervals • Market makers take opposite side to outside demand imbalances and carry inventory • Nevertheless, the main business of market makers is to make money from bid-ask spreads

• Market makers must manage their risks very carefully since they carry large inventory risks and could be adversely selected by informed traders • Market makers will hedge against factors affecting options prices

Hedging against factors affecting options prices

How to do that? • Each individual factor is represented by a Greek letter • ∆𝑓 = 𝑇ℎ𝑒𝑡𝑎 × ∆𝑡 + 𝐷𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 × ∆𝑆 + 𝑉𝑒𝑔𝑎 × ∆𝜎 + 𝑅ℎ𝑜 × ∆𝑟 + 1 𝐺𝑎𝑚𝑚𝑎 × (∆𝑆) 2 • We want a situation when an option value won’t change when one of the factors change → we want all Greek letters to be zero • We can do that by making certain transactions in underlying assets, futures on these assets, and options • First, we will consider hedging against changes in each individual factor separately

Delta hedging

• Suppose a financial institution has sold a European call option on 100,000 shares of a non-dividend paying stock. How does the financial institution hedge its risk? 𝜕𝑓 • Recall construction of a risk-free portfolio consisting of -1 derivative and 𝑑𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 = ∂S shares of a stock • This portfolio value doesn’t change as the price of the underlying asset changes • We will need to buy delta number of stocks, so that our portfolio value doesn’t change as the price of the underlying stock changes • We call this hedging “delta hedging”

Example: a single option position

A bank has sold for $300,000 a European call option on 100,000 shares of a non-dividend paying stock

S0 = 49, K = 50, r = 5%, s = 20%, T = 20 weeks, m = 13%

How does the bank hedge its risk?

Some hedging strategies ▪ Naked position Take no action ▪ Covered position Buy 100,000 shares today and hold ▪ Stop-Loss strategy ▪ Buying 100,000 shares as soon as price reaches $50 ▪ Selling 100,000 shares as soon as price falls below $50

▪ What is the risk associated with these strategies? ▪ What is the cost?

Stop-Loss Strategy

What is the problem with this strategy?

Delta-hedging Delta-hedging means to periodically adjust the number of shares the hedger holds to hedge the current sensitivity of the option to the price of the underlying

Goal is to be delta-neutral, i.e. the value of the combined portfolio does not change as a result of share price changes

Remember from the binomial tree, the hedge ratio between two future states (nodes): cu − cd c =  Su − Sd S In the limit, with infinitely many, very small steps in the tree, delta becomes the partial derivative of the price of the derivative (portfolio π) w.r.t. the price of the underlying

 = S

Example S0 = 49, K = 50, r = 5%, s = 20%, T = 20 weeks = .3846 years

 = N (d1 )  ln   S0   K + r −q+ 2 T s2 ( ) where d1 = =  = .0542 s T i.e. N (d1 ) = 0.5220

Delta of futures contracts

• Consider the value of a long futures contract on a non-dividend paying stock: 𝑓 = 𝐹𝑡 − 𝐹0 = 𝑆 𝑡𝑒−𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 − 𝐹0 • The delta of this futures contract value is 𝜕𝑓 = 𝑒−𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 • That is, a short futures contract, can be hedged by purchasing 𝑒 −𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 shares, while a long futures contract can be hedged by shorting 𝑒−𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 shares • You can also apply delta to underlying prices by assuming that 𝑓 = 𝑆: 𝜕𝑓 • = 𝜕𝑆 = 1 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑆 • That is, a short position in underlying can be hedged by purchasing 1 share of the underlying, and vice versa

Delta of European call options

• X = 50, r = 10%, (q = 0), T = ½ year, σ = 0.40 = 40% 50

• Non-dividend-paying stock: S = 50, X = 50, r = 5%, σ = 0.30, T = 3 months ln 𝑆 +(𝑟+1 𝜎 2 )(𝑇−𝑡) ln 50 + 0.05+ 10.30 2 3/12 • 𝑑1 = 𝑋 2 = 2 = 0.16 𝜎 𝑇−𝑡 0.30 3/12 • 𝐷𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 = ∆= 𝑁 0.16 = 0.5636 • So, if you sell one call you can hedge by buying 0.56 shares • Also, if the stock price changes by $1, the call option price changes by DeltadS = (0.5636)$1 = $0.5636 • Here we assume that all other variables are constant (in the Taylor’s theorem)

P(t) delta = 𝜕𝑝 at S = $50 20 • For European options on non-DIVpaying stocks ∆= 𝜕𝑐 = 𝑁(−𝑑 1) 𝜕 • For other European options: ∆= 𝑒−𝑞 𝑇−𝑡 𝑁(−𝑑1) 0 • Where q equals to the dividend yield, -5 0 20 40 60 80 100 the foreign risk-free rate, or just the risk-free rate depending on the type of the underlying asset  Using futures for delta hedging

• People often use futures contracts rather than the underlying asset to hedge options • The futures contract does not have to expire at the same time as the options contract • Define: • S = price of the underlying asset • f = price of the option on S • T = maturity of the option • T* = maturity of the futures on S ∗ • F* = the futures price: 𝐹∗ = 𝑆𝑒(𝑟−𝑞)(𝑇 −𝑡) • HS = required position in the underlying asset at time t for delta hedging = 𝜕𝑓 𝜕 • HF* = required position in the futures contract at time t for delta hedging = 𝜕𝐹 ∗

Using futures for delta hedging – cont.

• How do HS and HF* are related to each other? • The easiest way to see is through the chain rule from calculus: 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝐹∗ 𝜕𝐹∗ (𝑟−𝑞)(𝑇 ∗−𝑡) 𝐻𝑆 = = = 𝐻𝐹 ∗ = 𝐻 𝐹 ∗ 𝑒 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝐹∗ 𝜕𝑆 ∗ Since the futures price 𝐹∗ = 𝑆𝑒(𝑟−𝑞)(𝑇 −𝑡), 𝜕𝐹 = 𝑒(𝑟−𝑞)(𝑇 −𝑡) ∗ ∗ • 𝜕 • So 𝐻𝐹 ∗ = 𝐻𝑆 𝑒 −(𝑟−𝑞)(𝑇 ∗−𝑡)

• So, we can hedge an option position with an underlying asset or with the futures on the underlying asset • Why do you think we would want to use futures over underlying assets (or vice versa) for hedging?

Delta of a portfolio

• Let’s go back to a market maker • A market maker has positions in many options on the same underling asset, but different strike prices and maturities • Deltas of option positions on the same underlying asset are additive • Suppose you have a portfolio of options and other derivatives on the same underlying asset • If you purchase (or sell) ni of the I th option, and if the value (price) of the I th option fisi, then the value of the portfolio, П, is: П = 𝑛1𝑓1 + 𝑛2𝑓2 + ⋯ + 𝑛𝑁𝑓𝑁 = σ 𝑁𝑖 = 𝑛𝑖𝑓𝑖 𝜕𝑓 1 • If the delta of the ith option is ∆ = , then ∆ 𝑖 𝑵 П = 𝒏𝟏 ∆𝟏 + 𝒏𝟐 ∆𝟐 + ⋯ + 𝒏𝑵 ∆𝑵 = σ 𝒊=𝟏 𝒏𝒊 ∆ 𝒊 𝜕 • For market makers, there is an economy of scale from offsetting positions (in terms of delta)

Delta of a portfolio – example

• Suppose you write 100 calls, each with delta_1 = 0.56, and write 50 calls, each with delta_2 = 0.30, and you buy 80 shares of the underlying stock • All options are on the same underlying asset • What is the Delta of your portfolio?

• The value of the portfolio: • ∆П= 𝑛1∆1 + 𝑛2∆2 + 𝑛3∆𝑆= −100 0.56 − 50 0.30 + 80 1 = 9 • If the underlying stock price increases by $1, your portfolio value will increase by $9 • You would sell 9 stocks out of your portfolio to make it delta-neutral

Practical aspects of delta hedging

• Delta hedging is costly • It effectively involves a “buy high sell low” trading rule • Transaction costs (commissions, bid-ask spreads, etc.) • Short-selling costs (of the underlying asset) • In practice, delta hedge is needed at least once a day, depending on trading costs • Options market makers want to make their portfolio as delta-neutral as possible and would monitor their portfolio delta to be close to zero at all times more or less • Option market makers are in the business of making money from bid-ask spreads in options, they don’t want changes in the underlying asset to ruin their profits

Theta

• The theta of a derivative (or a portfolio of derivatives), , is the rate of change of the value of the portfolio with respect to time with all else remaining the same 𝜕П = 𝜕𝑡 • The theta of call or put is usually negative, i.e., ceteris paribus, the value of a long call or put option declines over time • Conversely, writers of options gain from the passage of time • Think of theta as the time decay of the portfolio value • When you calculate theta using the given formulas, it’s measured in $ per year, but market participants usually prefer to see a time decay per day • So if theta for a call option equals $6, it means this option loses $6/365 = $0.0164 of its time value every day when you buy it

Theta of European call options

• X = 50, r = 10%, (q = 0), T = ½ 60 year, σ = 0.40 = 40% • The bold curve is for T - t = 1 year, and the dotted curve is for T - t = 40 ½ year • As the time to maturity decreases 30

the graph of c decreases towards the intrinsic value, max{0, St - X}. 20 • For European call options on nondividend paying stocks: S−X 𝑆0 𝑁′(𝑑1)𝜎 = − 𝑟𝑋𝑒 −𝑟 (𝑇−𝑡 𝑁(𝑑2) 0 2 𝑇−𝑡 ) 1 2 0 20 40 60 80 100 • where 𝑁 ′ 𝑥 = 𝑒−2𝑥 -10 2𝜋  Theta of European put options

• Theta for a European put on a nonDIV paying stock can be positive 20 • The reason for this is that an ITM put 18 can be worth less than its intrinsic 16 value, max{0, X - St}. 14 • For European put options on non- 12 dividend paying stocks: 𝑆0 𝑁′(𝑑1)𝜎 10 = + 𝑟𝑋𝑒 −𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 𝑁(−𝑑2) 8 2 𝑇−𝑡 1 1 2 6 • where 𝑁 ′ 𝑥 = 𝑒−2𝑥 2𝜋 0 10 20 30 40   Thetas and strike prices

Gamma

• The gamma, , of a portfolio of derivatives on the same underlying asset is the rate of change of the delta with respect to the price of the underlying asset: 𝜕∆ 𝜕 2 П = = 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑆 2 • For European call or put without dividends: 𝑵′(𝒅𝟏) = 𝑺𝟎𝝈 𝑻 − 𝒕 where 𝑁 ′ 𝑥 = 1 𝑒−2𝑥 2 2𝜋

Gamma and delta

• Gamma measures the error from delta hedging that’s caused by the curvature of the relationship between S and the option price

0 20 40 60 80 100 -10  Gamma – example

• Suppose your option’s Delta is 0 = 0.50, and its Gamma is  = 0.030. Then if the stock’s price increases by $2, the delta of the option increases by 0.060 to 1 = 0.56: ∆1= ∆0 + 𝑑∆= 0.50 + 0.030 2 = 0.56 • If you have written the call and if you want to be delta neutral, then you must increase your holding in the underlying asset from 0.50 shares to 0.56 shares • What is the delta of the option if S decreases by $2?

Delta-Gamma hedging

• Consider Taylor’s theorem again: 𝜕П 1 𝜕2 П 2 1 2 𝑑П ≈ 𝑑𝑆 + 𝑑𝑆 = 𝐷𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 𝑑𝑆 + 𝐺𝑎𝑚𝑚𝑎 𝑑𝑆 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆 2 2 • Example • Using this second order approximation, find the change in the value of the option with 0 = 0.50 and  = 0.030 when the stock’s price increases by $2 𝑑П ≈ 0.50 $2 + 0.030 $4 = $1.06 • What’s the change when the stock price decreases by $2? 𝑑П ≈ 0.50 −$2 + 0.030 $4 = −$0.94

Delta-Gamma hedging

• If you want your portfolio’s value to remain unchanged when S changes, you may want a Delta neutral and Gamma neutral portfolio • To have a Gamma neutral portfolio, you will need to use options on the same underlying asset • Other instruments – the underlying asset itself of futures on this assets –can’t neutralize portfolio gamma because second derivatives of these instruments’ functions w.r.t. S are zero

Delta-Gamma hedging – example

• There are two calls on the same underlying asset with 1 = 0.50, 1 = 0.03, and 2 = 0.40 and 2 = 0.025. Suppose you write 100 of the call #1. Make this portfolio deltagamma neutral without changing your current position in call options #1 • ∆П= 𝑛1∆1= −100 0.50 = −50 • П = 𝑛11 = −100 0.03 = −3 • First, make your portfolio gamma neutral by adding option #2 in our portfolio • П = 𝑛11 + 𝑛22 = 0 ⇒ −3 + 𝑛2 0.025 = 0 ⇒ 𝑛2 = +120 • You need to buy 120 of the call #2 to make your portfolio gamma equal to zero • New portfolio delta: ∆П= 𝑛1∆1 + 𝑛2∆2= −50 + 120 0.40 = −2 • You need to buy 2 underlying stocks to make your portfolio delta-neutral • Notice that when you do it, it doesn’t affect your portfolio gamma because 𝑆 = 0 • It’s important to neutralize your portfolio w.r.t.  first, and only then w.r.t. ∆

Accuracy of delta-gamma approximation • The second order approximation 𝑑П ≈ 𝜕П 𝑑𝑆 + 1 𝜕 П 𝑑𝑆 2 = 50 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆 2 𝐷𝑒𝑙𝑡𝑎 𝑑𝑆 + 𝐺𝑎𝑚𝑚𝑎 𝑑𝑆 2 is quite 2 40 accurate for reasonable changes in the stock price, dS, as this graph 30 illustrates • Here, S0 = $50 20

Vega

• Vega is the rate of change of the value of the portfolio of options with respect to the volatility 𝜕П 𝑉𝑒𝑔𝑎 = 𝜕𝜎 • For European options without dividends: 𝑽𝒆𝒈𝒂 = 𝑺𝟎 𝑻 − 𝒕𝑵′(𝒅𝟏) • Interpretation: say we have a call option on non-dividend paying stock with 𝑆0 = 48, 𝜎 = 0.20, 𝑇 − 𝑡 = 0.3846, 𝑁 ′ 𝑑1 = 0.3984, so that vega = 12.1 • It means that for a 1% change in volatility (from 20% to 21%) the call price increases by 0.01(12.1) = $0.121

Vega • The following graph illustrates the effect of Vega 50 • The bold line is for σ= 0.4, while the dotted line corresponds to σ= 0.2 40 • The greatest difference is for at-the- 30 money options (K = 50)

Excel example

• We can make our portfolio delta, gamma, and vega neutral if we add two options to our portfolio • Let’s look at the options on oil futures. The market maker used Black-Scholes formulas to calculate all theoretical prices and Greeks. In the middle of the day the portfolio of this market maker consists of: 100 long call options with K = 360, 30 long call options with K = 370, and 50 long put options with K = 440. Make your portfolio delta-gamma-vega neutral. See whiteboard notes Work it in Excel

Rho

• The Rho of a portfolio of derivatives is the rate of change of the value of the portfolio with respect to the interest rate: 𝜕П 𝑅ℎ𝑜 = 𝜕𝑟 • For European call options: 𝑹𝒉𝒐 = 𝑲 𝑻 − 𝒕 𝒆−𝒓 𝑻−𝒕 𝑵 𝒅𝟐 > 𝟎 • For European put options: 𝑹𝒉𝒐 = −𝑲 𝑻 − 𝒕 𝒆−𝒓 𝑻−𝒕 𝑵 −𝒅𝟐 < 𝟎

Rho and strike prices

What is the relationship between rho and moneyness of the option (its strike price)?

The Greeks for all European options

q = dividend yield for options on dividend paying stocks, q = rf for options on currencies, q = r for options on futures

The Greeks for American options?

• Many options, e.g., CBOE equity options, are American. How to calculate and manage Greeks for these options? • It is possible to do that with binomial trees • You have formula for calculating delta already: ∆= 𝑓𝑢−𝑓𝑑 𝑆𝑢−𝑆𝑑 • For other Greeks, you calculate American option price with one parameter value • And then change this parameter by 1 unit and re-calculate an American option price • The change in the American option price represents the according Greek – the parameter that you’re changing

Greeks and moneyness: summary

• Delta: approaches 0 as the option goes deep OTM, and approaches ±1 as it goes deep ITM • Theta: is highest for ATM American options, for European the relation is less clear • Gamma: is highest for ATM options, and approaches 0 as the option goes deep OTM or ITM • Vega: is highest for ATM options, and approaches 0 as the option goes deep OTM or ITM • Rho: is highest (in magnitude) for deep ITM options and approaches zero as the option goes deep OTM

These relations are important because each Greek represents some risk. Options with different moneyness have different risks → market makers would require higher compensation for buying and selling these options  Portfolio insurance

• You can synthetically create a protective put for your portfolio by matching  • This involves selling enough of the portfolio to match the  of the put option. • To see how this works, let us look at the price of a European put option (let t = 0): Put = Xe-rTN(-d2) – Se-qTN(-d1) = “cash” – S|  | • Essentially, it means that an option can be replicated by a risk-free asset (“cash”) and the underlying asset • For a protective put, we would like to (synthetically) buy this put; that is, we want to replicate a long put. So we must • Buy “cash”, i.e., buy the risk-free asset, and • Sell || shares

Portfolio insurance

• If the value of the portfolio increases,  becomes less negative, so we sell less of our portfolio • If the value of the portfolio decreases,  becomes more negative and more of the portfolio must be sold. • In other words, as the market drops, you want to sell more of your holdings

• This strategy didn’t work well on October 19, 1987…
